text: **Translation:** The Record of Strange Events (Ming Dynasty) by Zhu Yunming Recording strange events may not be as beneficial as recording ordinary matters, yet mysterious and strange occurrences are an inevitable part of the universe. Unusual events are beyond ordinary human thought and consideration. If one can record the reality of such events, then those who encounter them suddenly will know how to avoid or evade them, and such records can serve as a means of encouragement and punishment, thus having some benefit. Moreover, such stories can be eye-catching and alarming, and they are enjoyable to discuss and hear. In the past, Hong's *Yijian Zhi* consisted of 420 volumes. If he did not find joy in it, why did he continue writing for so long? I understand that my book, though vulgar and crude, cannot compare with Hong's work, but I am simply following my own interests. If those with lofty opinions blame my work for its absurdity and dismiss it as a failure to speak of ordinary things, then *Yijian Zhi* should be dismissed first, and I have no reason to worry.

陈僖敏俞宫保 陈僖敏公镒与太子太保俞公士悦年轻时经常一起出入，关系密切。一天清晨，他们一同进入郡学，旁边一位居民的门内听到街上枷锁的声音，探头一看，只见狱卒带着几名囚犯向南行进。不久，狱卒和囚犯们仓皇地说：“丞相来了，应该赶快避开！”于是他们都进入了一座古庙消失了。这位居民心想囚犯不应该走这条路，而且对丞相的话感到惊讶，便坐下等待。不久，陈僖敏公和俞公士悦也到了。 还娘 泰和萧都宪的儿媳刘氏，十七岁时嫁给萧家，名字叫还娘。突然得了病去世了。萧家从家里说起这件事，不到十天就将她葬在城外。还娘刚进入冥府时，看到三位王者坐在那里，其中一位看了看还娘说：“这是个错误，该怎么办呢？”官吏禀报王者，希望暂时留下她。但王者坚持不允许，于是将她放回人间。途中，她看到一位邻居妇女也被追来。还娘问她为什么，邻居妇女说：“因为我的女儿擅自杀了鹅，被我用棍子打死了，她上诉冤屈，被追到这里。”还娘被关在棺材里，无法出来，忽然听到坟上群鸟鸣叫，有人看见觉得很奇怪，来到墓旁听到墓中有人呼喊的声音，告诉萧家，萧家急忙打开坟墓，将她救了出来。她非常疲惫，便加以救治，逐渐恢复了健康。计算她死去的时间是十七天。当时邻居妇女打婢女时，还娘已经死了几天，由此可知她的话并非虚言。

In the spring of the Renyin year during the Chenghua era (1465), a severe epidemic swept through the Wu region, particularly affecting the rural areas. In the village of Wuhuanjing, a family of seven died together, leaving no survivors. There was no one to bury them. A coffin maker in the market met an old man who came to his door to buy seven coffins. The old man had no money and was destitute. He promised the coffin maker, "Just carry me and the coffins home, and I will pay you." The coffin maker agreed and took the old man and the coffins to his house. Upon arrival, they found the house empty. Inside the room, they saw seven corpses. The coffin maker was horrified and went to find the old man's neighbor, who lived in the northwest of the village. The neighbor was also shocked and sad. The old man's promise was fulfilled, and the neighbor paid the coffin maker with twenty shi of wheat.

### 天堕草船 In the west of Songjiang City, there lived a man named Dong Zhongfu, who was known for his virtue and integrity. On the 11th day of the eighth month in the year Chenghua, the sky was clear and bright, with no trace of clouds. People saw a small boat floating in the air, moving from east to west and then back to the east before finally falling. Those watching from the upper floor of Dong Zhongfu's house were packed together. Upon closer inspection, it was found to be made of reeds and grass. At the time, Dong Zhongfu was suffering from deafness in his ears. He was not greatly alarmed but said, "This boat has come to take me." Soon after, he passed away as predicted. ### 白犬怪 In Kunshan, there was a young man from a common family who had not yet married. One night, a beautiful woman came to him and they had relations. As a result, he contracted a wasting disease, and medical treatment could not cure him. The son still kept the matter secret. One day, while sitting in a shop, a Taoist priest passed by and said to someone, "This young man has a very strong aura of evil." After saying this, he left. The father heard this and then told his son, "If you don't speak, you will die. If you tell me, I won't blame you." The son then told his father the truth. The father said, "I will give you a pair of scissors. When she comes, just cut her clothes, and you can verify the cure." The son accepted the command with great care. That night, the woman came, and the son secretly cut her clothes and shouted at her fiercely. The woman then left. In the morning, it was found that she had white hair. The father saw a white female dog at the neighbor's house with a missing patch of fur. They then beat the dog to death together. The son's illness then improved.

冯副使 In my hometown, there was a Deputy Magistrate named Feng, who during the Chenghua period, after attending court and returning home, went directly to the Imperial Clan Court. The court had an ancient well that was very large. Suddenly, Feng dismounted and approached the well, bowing several times towards its depths. The servants and those who saw him were just startled when Feng suddenly unfastened his belt and placed it on the ground, then jumped into the water. They tried to save him, but he had already died. Along the way, Feng had called a flute player, who was pleased to be traveling with him because of his skill with the flute. Upon arriving in the capital, the flute player begged to leave, and Feng, in anger, beat him to death. At this point, people began to speak of Feng as "Feng the Ghost-Catcher." 贡尚书 In the sixteenth year of the Chenghua era, Luo Xima Jing and Li Shijiang Eastyang jointly presided over the autumn examination in the southern region. While staying at the examination hall, Li had a dream in which a spirit appeared, saying, "Grand Secretary Gong is coming to see you." Li invited him in, and the spirit turned out to be a noble official, very dignified in appearance, wearing a ceremonial cap and robe. The official said to Li, "I have a young grandson who is well-read and has come to take the exam. I wish for you to select him." Li humbly declined. After waking up, Li told Luo about the dream, but Luo, who had never heard of such a person, did not think much of it. Later, when the results were announced, Li had another dream in which the noble official came to thank him, saying, "My grandson has been selected, and I come to express my gratitude." Li woke up still puzzled. When the exam papers were reviewed, the first choice was indeed Gong Qin from Ningguo. It turned out that Gong Qin was the grandson of the former Grand Secretary Shi Tai. 王捉鬼 A villager from Xiangcheng named Wang Lang was known for his courage. One night, he opened the door and saw something in the dark, suspecting it to be a ghost. He immediately went forward to catch it, shouting, "The ghost is here!" The household lit a torch and looked, and they found that it was just a rotten coffin lid. Wang burned it immediately, and the sound was like firecrackers. After a long time, the villagers began to call him "Wang the Ghost-Catcher."

The Sea God Invites a Reader In the east gate of Jiading, there lived a thirteen-year-old son of a magistrate named Zhu. One day, he was carrying his book bag to meet his private tutor and leaned against the door for a moment. Suddenly, he rushed eastward like the wind and disappeared in an instant. His parents quickly gathered the family and neighbors to chase after him, but they could not catch up. A passerby said that he had just run past, grabbed a tree to stop, but could not do so, and the tree broke as he continued on his way. Another person reported that he had rushed straight toward the sea, and then came news that he had already entered the sea. The parents had no idea what to do and wept bitterly by the water's edge for three days. Just as they were about to return, a person suddenly emerged from the water, and upon seeing it was their son, they were about to embrace him, but he continued westward like the wind. The parents hurried back home, but there was no one there. They were both terrified and heartbroken. The next day, a beggar came to report that the previous night, he had seen a child lying on the ground and sleeping in the village land temple. Upon closer inspection, it was indeed their son. The parents were overjoyed and rushed with the beggar to the temple, where they found him lying on the ground. They helped him up and brought him home. He was still dazed and unconscious, suffering from a severe illness. Fortunately, he opened his eyes and could move, and they fed him with soup. After one day, he returned to normal. Everyone asked for details, and he said, "I was leaning against the door when I suddenly saw a young man coming from the east. He had a very beautiful appearance, wearing a soft, wide-brimmed hat, a green robe, and a golden belt on the left side. He was riding a white horse, also as bright and white as snow. Following him were about thirty people, all with human bodies but heads that were either shrimp, snails, turtles, or fish. The young man saw me and ordered his followers to carry me eastward, and even if I shouted, I could not stop."

Upon reaching the shore, they saw a path through the sea, which soon became a road leading to the sand. The crowd followed this path, and in an instant, they arrived at a city. They entered a grand palace with red doors and splendid buildings, wide and magnificent. A young man stood at the gate, only allowing the gatekeeper to pass through. He said, "I am here on command to invite scholars." The gatekeeper, who was also a fish or turtle, received the message and led them inside. After a short while, he came out and sent another official to guide them to a hall below the palace. The hall was extremely high and wide, all made of white material, resembling the shells of turtles and oysters, with dazzling light that almost blinded them. The official then led them into the hall, where the king sat on the throne. He appeared old, with white hair and beard, wearing a tall hat and a white robe embroidered with gold patterns, and a white jade belt around his waist. He asked, "Do you know how to write articles?" I replied, "I do not." He then asked, "What can you do?" I answered, "I can only make rhymes." The king said, "I only need those who can write articles. Since you cannot, you are of no use to me." He then ordered the attendants to take me to a school for a visit and then let me return. The attendants then led me to a room on the east side, where they showed me a young boy, about five or six years old, exceptionally beautiful and unique. An attendant explained that the king wanted to invite scholars to teach this boy. After a short while, they led me back to the king, who ordered them to send me away again. The crowd then led me out, where the young man was still waiting at the gate. Upon receiving the order, I got back on my horse, and the crowd helped me return. We arrived at a land temple, where the temple deity greeted us with great respect. The young man then handed the child over to the land deity, who took the child and kept him. The child had no further issues, and he is still alive today. This event took place in the nineteenth year of the Chenghua era.

### 雷书 During the months of May and June in the year of Ding Wei, Wu region suffered a severe drought, with lush grass growing at the riverbed and a thousand miles of land turning barren. On the twenty-fifth day of July, a sudden and intense thunderstorm occurred. That day, at the hour of Shen, there was a catalpa tree with a girth of several arms near the East Neighbor of Chongyi An. A large branch of the tree was struck by lightning. Later, when examining the ground beneath the tree, they found characters written across it, measuring about five to six inches in width and over two feet in length, with lines as broad as a finger and deeply embedded in the soil. The text read: "This is not seal script or cursive script. Even after being washed by rain, it did not disappear. It was written by a heavenly deity." The son of the Zhang family, who was my friend, told me about this the next day. I then copied the text onto paper. --- ### 妓乘鱼 In the music bureau of Nanjing, there was a courtesan who had a very close relationship with a salt merchant. The merchant traveled to Guangling and said to the courtesan, "I will be returning soon. Can you wait for me?" The courtesan agreed. However, the merchant never returned. Despite the courtesan's door being desolate, she did not change her feelings. She searched for the merchant in many ways and eventually found his whereabouts. She then hurriedly went to find him, and indeed, she met him. The merchant was moved by her loyalty and gave her a great amount of gold and silk, saying, "My affairs are not yet completed, and I will be leaving in a few days. You may return now. This gift is not enough to express my gratitude. When I return, I will continue our previous relationship." The courtesan bought a boat and returned alone. She took some of the gold and instructed the boatman to break it, using it to buy things. The boatman secretly thought he could gain something from this. At night, while on the river, he attempted to take advantage of the situation.

**First Part:** The courtesan threw herself into the water, and all her goods were there. She arrived at a village in Jiangyin as the sun was rising and shouted for help. People in the village gathered to see what had happened. In an instant, they reached the shore, and the crowd helped her up from the water. They saw that she was riding on a large fish, and it was calculated that the thieves had not yet seen this. The official acted as she said and quickly went to check all the boats. He indeed caught the thieves and punished them according to the law, then sent the courtesan back to her quarters. **Second Part:** In the Fushan area of Changshu, there was a farmer who had a child named Bao Bao. Bao Bao was physically weak and could not walk until he was four or five years old. He spent his days sitting on a wooden bench and was good at predicting people's fortunes. When asked, he always responded accurately. His family was puzzled by this but also benefited from it, becoming more prosperous. One day, a Taoist priest from Longhu Mountain passed by their door and told people, "This family must have an extraordinary creature." Wealthy man Liu Yize heard this and summoned the priest, asking if he could eliminate it. The priest replied, "This is not difficult." Liu then prepared a boat and sent him on his way. As the priest was traveling, Bao Bao suddenly told his mother, "There is a Taoist priest coming; I am going to die. Give him ten thousand coins so I can beg for my life." His mother was puzzled, but the priest was already on the road. He secretly wrote a cinnabar talisman and burned it at the root of a willow tree. Bao Bao was already at home, vomiting blood. Soon after, he burned another talisman, and a large fish died on the surface of the water, looking strong and fearsome, with a head as big as a fist. The priest asked the family if their son had died.

**Translation:** **A Woman Giving Birth Through a Split in Her Abdomen** In Suizhou, Fengyang Prefecture, a woman named Wang, wife of Zhang, was pregnant and due to give birth. She experienced unbearable pain on the right side of her navel, which lasted for three months, leaving her extremely exhausted. On the first day of the third month, during the hour of the pig in the tenth year of the Chénghuà era, a split suddenly opened on the right side of her abdomen, and she gave birth to a boy. The boy had a black mole on the tip of his nose. The official provisions were provided by the censor-general Zhou Fan, who reported the matter to the court. I personally saw Zhou and heard him say the following. **The Lotus Monk** In Sichuan, there was a large bridge where a monk lived. The monk was always cautious and virtuous, never idle or negligent. He would weave straw sandals and hang them on the bridge, allowing passersby to take them if they wished. From this, one can infer his other activities. One day, it rained heavily, and a loud thunderclap struck. When they looked, the monk's skull had split and he had died. Everyone thought it was a mistake, and some suspected he had hidden evil. However, his chest and abdomen were not cold. His disciples did not bury him for five days, and then he revived, saying, "When I was first struck, I was already dead. The thunder god exclaimed, 'It was a mistake!' and quickly ordered medicine to treat me. He also ordered the ghosts to rub and mend the broken place. After a long time, it was restored to its original state, and I returned to life. I did not know I had been dead for five days. When I looked at my scalp, it was still connected, but my bones were broken into eight-sided pieces. People called me the Lotus Monk." The old man Wu in Sichuan witnessed this event. **The Sky Splitting** My father said that during the Xuande era, while I was at the schoolhouse, I went to the county for some matter. At the time between noon and 3 PM, I suddenly saw the sky split in the southwest, for more than ten zhang. The sky was clear and blue with no clouds, and inside it was vast and dark, seemingly endless. After a long time, it closed. At the time, hundreds of people, including my peers and those before and after the government office, all saw it.

**Xie the Old Ox** In Suzhou, outside the阊门 on Shangtang Street, the Yang family, who were in the oil business, kept an ox for grinding oil for eighteen years. Because the ox was old and declining, they decided to sell it. They called a person to discuss the price, and just before the deal was finalized, the ox appeared in the person's dream, saying, "I am Xie Tiaopan from Shixiang. I bore the debt of the Yang family and served as a cow to repay it. Now that I am old, the owner is about to sell me to you. Do not buy me!" The person woke up from the dream, but the Yang family did not call him back. A few days later, the ox appeared in the person's dream again, saying, "The owner will call you tomorrow. They will kill me the day after tomorrow. I have no enmity with you, so do not buy me!" It then said, "I am in the fourth pen of the Yang family. I have white hair and a wrinkled neck skin because of the grinding. You should recognize me." After speaking, it wept. The person then agreed not to buy the ox. Upon waking, they all sighed and expressed surprise. The Yang family indeed came to call him, but when they saw the ox in the fourth pen, it was exactly as seen in the dream. When the person came to see it, the ox bent its two legs to the ground and wept with its face covered in tears. The Yang family was very surprised and the person explained the reason, further advising the Yang family not to kill the ox. The Yang family was also greatly surprised and pledged not to sell it. However, the Xie family learned about it and came to redeem it, keeping it until the end of its life. This was during the spring of the eighteenth year of the Chenghua era. **Old Man He Sells a Ghost Sheep** In Changzhou, there was an old man named He who once went into the city to perform a task. It was still dark when he saw a sheep on the road. He suspected it and thought, "It is too late at night to have a sheep here. This must be a ghost!" He carried it along without stopping. The sheep moaned and begged to be released, but he did not listen. When it was light, he realized it was a real sheep and sold it at a shop for five coins. In the evening, he returned to look for the buyer but the sheep was gone. He told the truth and returned the money.

### Final Translation **叶宗可** In the late Yuan dynasty, Ye Zongke fled to Huaiyang to escape turmoil. When bandits arrived, corpses piled up across the fields. Ye Zongke hid during the day and traveled at night. When he could no longer proceed, he lay on the ground among the crowd of corpses. At night, under the bright moon, he saw a person approaching with a lantern, leading the way. As he drew near, he recognized a Taoist, accompanied by a young boy holding a candle. They reached the side of the corpses. The Taoist lit the candle and examined the corpses, discarding all women, elderly men, children, the weak, the crippled, and the disabled, as if they were mere leaves. Soon, he found a strong man with a robust body. The Taoist scrutinized him closely and, pleased, immediately took off his clothes and embraced the corpse. They held each other tightly, and the Taoist breathed into the corpse's mouth. After a long time, the Taoist's breath grew faint, and the corpse slowly stirred. Soon, the corpse yawned, opened his eyes, and pushed the Taoist to the ground. He leaped up suddenly, still ordering the young boy to hold the candle and lead the way, then departed lightly, disappearing without a trace. **张道士** In Suzhou, the Taoist Zhang Zongmao of the Xuanmiao Temple's Thunder Hall was known for his humility, gentleness, and ease. He practiced the Five Thunder Techniques with great efficacy. Whenever there was drought or flood, the prefectures and counties would summon him for prayers, which were always answered promptly. In the third year of Chenghua, he was appointed as the Director of the Taoist Registry. I once wrote an article to honor him. In the first month of Dingwei, he resigned from his position due to illness, yet he appeared to be in good health. On the sixteenth day, he returned to the secular world, visiting all his family members. He said to them, "Zongmao is old. This journey is a farewell." He then entered the city to visit his friends, and some speculated that he was leaving, though others did not understand. He told his disciples, "I wish to depart." On the first day of May, he rose early, washed himself thoroughly, held incense, and went to the temple to perform a ritual bow. After completing it, he said, "I am bidding farewell to the Thunder God today." Soon, he returned to his room to pack his clothes.

The master distributed miscellaneous items among his disciples. Until nightfall, he silently sat on a small bed in the room. The next day, he wrapped incense in a silk handkerchief and visited each room to request an audience. The Daoists said, "Master Zongmao, today we bid farewell to you." Upon returning to his room, he sat upright without speaking. The disciples stood by, unwilling to leave. The master commanded them to go out, and when they returned to see him, they found that he had closed his eyes. They immediately shouted for help. The master opened his eyes and said, "Today is very good. You should not stay with me. You can bring me some ginger soup; I want to drink." The disciples hurried to fetch the soup. The master quickly got up, closed the door, and then sat on the bed and passed away. After a long time, the disciples came to call him, but he was already unresponsive. It was the sixteenth day of the day. He left a verse saying: "Sixty-four years of the bright moon, Within it there are full and waning phases. Today it flies into the sky, Penetrating the three realms and ten directions." --- During the Yongle period, there was Mr. Liu Xijiang, who loved to wander. One day, he arrived at a mountain temple where the monks were quite tired of him. The temple had two vacant rooms, and anyone who stayed there died. One day, Mr. Liu Xijiang came to the temple, and the monks prepared a feast for him. They encouraged him to drink until he was drunk and then went to sleep in the room. At the second drum strike, he woke up from his wine and heard someone reciting the *Guanyin Sutra*. The sound gradually approached. Mr. Liu Xijiang thought it was the monks' voices and got up, dressing himself. At that moment, the moon outside the window was as bright as day. Mr. Liu Xijiang looked through the window crack and saw a beautiful woman. She finished reciting and bowed her hands toward the window. Suddenly, the window opened, and the woman entered, sitting in the upper seat. Soon, she extended her tongue to a length of over a meter, threatening to approach Mr. Liu Xijiang. He was in a panic and threw a blanket at her. The woman seemed to vomit and then disappeared. In the morning, Mr. Liu Xijiang thanked the monks and left. As he passed by the side of the mountain, he saw a giant snake with its tongue extended, dead. Mr. Liu Xijiang said, "This is a snake spirit. It was the woman I saw last night. If it weren't for me, it would have eaten her." He told the monks about it, and the monks were ashamed and thanked him.

**Yang Libu Xunji's Father's Dream** Yang Libu Xunji, before passing the exams, his father dreamed that someone told the young master to come in 54th place. After waking up, he did not take it seriously. Later, he passed the provincial, metropolitan, and palace examinations and came in 18th place in each. He thought the previous dream was not reliable. Later, he reflected on it and combined the number of three attempts, which equaled 54. --- **The Zunjing Pavilion** The Zunjing Pavilion in Suzhou, built during the Song Dynasty, is very grand and solid. It is said that there are ghosts in the pavilion, and few people can ascend it. During the Xuande era, there was a rascal who made a pact with someone to sleep alone on the pavilion at night. By morning, nothing had happened, so the group should give him money. The rascal quickly hid on the beam and saw five men sitting upright facing forward. The attendants immediately served wine and food laid out on the table. The dishes, fruits, and nuts were abundant, rich, and clean. The drinking vessels were yellow and white, interspersed and full on the table. At dawn, they were about to disperse, and the rascal shouted to scare them. The ghosts fled at once, and nothing was seen. The objects were scattered on the table, and there was no time to tidy up. The rascal was greatly overjoyed and took all the objects down. The group came to trace them, and the rascal told them the truth. Soon, it was reported that the Qian family from Leqiao lost some gold, silver, and wine vessels at night. The rascal asked, "Are these the Qian family's possessions?" He took them to the Qian family, who saw them and recognized them as their own. The Qian family, being wealthy and pleased, returned them all.

### Translation **文秀才** Wen Zongyan, a scholar from Suzhou, was once studying at night on the upper floor of his house when he suddenly heard someone climbing the stairs. In a moment, a beautiful woman stood in front of his desk. Wen Zongyan sat motionless, and quickly took up the inkstone to hit her. She escaped with a hand movement, and nothing was seen. Later, he became a Grand Councillor. **测字** A few years ago, there was someone who came to inquire about the exam matters and wrote the character "串." The diviner said, "Not only will he pass the provincial exam, but he should also excel in the metropolitan exam." This is because the character "串" contains the meaning of "two passes." Another person was present and also wrote the character "串." The diviner said, "You will not only fail the exam but will also get sick." When asked why, the diviner explained, "He wrote it without intention, so it will be as it is written; you wrote it with intention, so it will be '患' (sickness)." Later, it turned out to be true. **鬼告状** During the reign of Xu Gongwu, who was managing the waterworks in Zhangqiu, there was an ironworker who finished his work and returned home. He passed by the side of Yue Wang Temple and met an old acquaintance, who realized he was dead and cursed him. The ghost was very angry and fought with him for a long time. Suddenly, Xu Gongwu's advance guards arrived, and the ironworker wanted to report the ghost, but the ghost pulled him to hide by the riverbank. The ironworker forcefully pulled him out, and the ghost held on tightly and could not be shaken off. The ghost said, "I dare not see Mr. Xu, so I will avoid him; but I can see the officials of the three boards." Later, the officials of the three boards arrived, and the ironworker shouted, and the ghost followed out. The officials of the three boards stopped and looked at it. The ironworker made a gesture with both hands and said, "The ghost is here." The ghost made a sound and said, "This person owes me 630 coins, and now he must pay me." There was a Censor named Liu who asked the ironworker, "Is this true?" The ironworker said, "Yes." Liu then told the ghost, "The ironworker owes you money, I will make him pay you back, so you can let go of him." The ghost immediately obeyed, and Liu advised the ironworker, who then burned many coins to appease the ghost, and everything was peaceful.

Omens of Success for Mr. Jiang in the Imperial Examinations When my mother's brother-in-law, Mr. Jiang Tinggui, was preparing to take the imperial examinations, he sought a sign from the diviners of Jiangdong and received the words "前三三后三三" (three before, three after). Initially, he did not understand what this meant. Later, he was unsuccessful in his three attempts. In the year 1471 during the reign of Emperor Chenghua, while staying at a lodging, he dreamed that the local schoolmaster, Mr. Chen Yu, took him to see a magnificent house and pointed out, saying, "This is the question for this year's examination." Mr. Jiang pondered this and realized it referred to the saying "富润屋之谓也" (wealth adorns the house), which suggests that wealth brings prosperity and honor. He then studied the topic more deeply. Eventually, the examination question was indeed "诚意全章" (sincerity and integrity), and he passed, ranking third. He published this explanation and wrote, "Initially, after Mr. Jiang's dream, he fell ill with malaria, which was very severe. He often cried out in distress, and his fellow lodgers asked him what was wrong. He replied, 'I saw three ghosts coming to disturb me.' One day, he leaped up and shouted loudly. They asked him what happened, and he said, 'I just saw a beautiful maiden who told me, "I am the Goddess of Osmanthus; do not be afraid."' People said this was a good omen. When he finally took the exam, he was forcibly carried into the examination hall. After passing, he thought about it and found that nothing failed to come true. The three ghosts were interpreted as the third place in the examination. Later, when he took the higher-level examinations at the Imperial Academy, he was unsuccessful in his first three attempts but eventually passed as the third place in the third rank of the Jinshi degree. This further confirmed the uniqueness of the sign. The numbers 'two' and 'three' in the sign referred to the number of attempts and the rank achieved, with not a single word being without meaning."

During the first year of the Hongzhi era in the spring, a Taoist priest at the Chao Tian Palace in Nanjing, who was just reaching the age of twenty, suddenly developed knee pain. Upon examination, his skin and flesh appeared shriveled, but the pain was not extremely severe. However, at night the pain doubled, and upon closer inspection, it was found to be a human-faced sore, with all parts of the face, including ears, eyes, nose, and mouth, fully present. The priest was extremely distressed and terrified. Whenever he encountered someone, he would show the sore to them, hoping to find a cure. However, whenever someone looked at it, the pain would penetrate to the bone marrow, and if no one was looking, the pain would subside. During the day, he could move around, wear clothes, and eat without much difficulty, but at night the pain would intensify, even if no one was watching. Suddenly, a person about two or three feet tall stood before him and spoke to him, saying, "Do you recognize me?" The priest replied, "No, I don't recognize you." The figure then said, "Don't you remember when I was a military commander in the Song dynasty? I was under your command. I had done nothing wrong, but you killed me out of personal resentment. And you also killed my entire family of seventeen, what a great injustice! I have been searching for you for three hundred years. Fortunately, I have found you now, how could I let you go?" The Taoist priest then died.

### Translation **Jichang** At the end of the Chenghua period, a family in Jiaxing gave birth to a boy who had red marks on his back resembling the words "Jichang" in faint, blood-like lines. The family thus named the boy after his birthmark. A few months later, villagers digging the ground found a blue stone slab with the words "Jichang" inscribed on it. Underneath the stone slab, there was a cellar containing hundreds of silver ingots, each also inscribed with the words "Jichang." Dozens of villagers vied to take the silver. The boy's father was also present and said, "My son has the words 'Jichang' on his body, and it is his name. I want to get all of it." The others did not agree but allowed him to take more, almost ten or six ingots. The family then became very wealthy. Others who obtained the silver suffered from illness and legal troubles that did not cease. Some who traded it also depleted themselves. One person who was quite wise said, "This is truly what heaven has given to that boy; it is not mine." He took it back, and the others also followed suit, giving everything to the boy's father. Thus, several families were spared from harm. The boy is still in the cradle, and it is unknown what will happen to him in the future. --- **The Craftsman of Nanjing** At the end of the Chenghua period, a craftsman from Nanjing went to work at the imperial palace and left at dusk through the San Shan Gate. He had only a worn coat, some coarse clothes, and a bundle of thirty coins tied together and carried on his back. He arrived at a remote place and met someone who said, "Where are you going, brother?" The craftsman replied, "I am taking a certain road home." The other person said, "I also want to go home this way. It is late, and we can travel together." The craftsman was very happy and went into an inn with him. They bought more wine and drank together. The craftsman was only grateful and comforted. They then shared a room for the night. The next day, they went together to a very secluded place. Suddenly, the other person pushed the craftsman to the ground, punched him, and kicked him. The craftsman, taken by surprise, could not stand up to defend himself and was beaten severely. He then fell unconscious and was thrown into a large moat, after which the other person ran away.

### Translation When he opened the cloth package, he saw only a tattered robe and a little money. He felt very regretful. When he got home, he opened the cloth package again and threw it on the bed. The tattered fur coat was hung on the beam, and the money was also temporarily placed under the table. Soon, there was a great storm. The craftsman was in the moat, but his breath had not yet ceased. He heard the sound of the wind and became slightly more awake. He saw a divine person who told him the name, residence, and location of the thief and the cloth package. The divine person also told him that the thief was sorry and would not leave home, and a certain official would pass by soon. He should shout and quickly go to catch him, and he would surely succeed. After the craftsman woke up, a patrol official indeed arrived. The craftsman shouted, and the official came out and asked him. He got the story and went straight to catch the thief, and the place where the clothes and money were found was exactly as described. Thus, the law was upheld. --- A Beautiful Woman by the Long Bridge There was an old woman by the side of the Chuihong Bridge in Wujiang who usually worked with an axe and sold skirts and hairpins. In the early autumn of the Hongzhi era, the old woman went out to the market, and a young girl stayed in the room. At that time, the slanting sun shone on the door, and the girl leaned on the door, playing with the lake light. She suddenly saw a beautiful woman in light blue clothes walking slowly. The girl was watching the beautiful woman, who then entered her house. The girl greeted and invited her to sit down, looking at her intently. The woman was still unmarried, but her appearance was very beautiful and her makeup was gorgeous. Her demeanor was graceful and captivating, and she seemed like a noblewoman's daughter. The girl dared not ask too many questions. The beautiful woman asked where the old woman was. The girl replied that she was out. The beautiful woman said, "I can't wait, I will come back to visit again." She then left by boat. The old woman returned, and the girl told her. The old woman was puzzled and did not expect it. The next day, when the old woman went out, the beautiful woman came again. The girl was surprised and greeted her, saying, "Mother has been waiting for you for a long time. You haven't come to see me, and you have something to do, so you went out." The beautiful woman said, "Yes." The girl then asked her name and surname, but she did not answer. The girl asked what the beautiful woman had to say. The beautiful woman hesitated for a long time, covered her sleeves, and spoke shyly.

The woman said, "I want to trouble you to find a match for me." The woman replied, "Then I will wait for my mother to return and tell her." The beautiful woman said, "Please remember this in your heart." She then pulled the woman and said, "My residence is not far away. Please send me there." The woman followed her. After a while, they saw a painted boat on the dike. The beautiful woman said, "This is my boat." She led the woman to board the boat together. The boat was adorned with precious and beautiful items, exuding a unique fragrance and a clear, bright light that made it seem unlike the human world. There was a small table with a bowl of dates on it. The beautiful woman took a few and offered them to the woman. The woman ate one or two and kept two in her pocket. The beautiful woman then sent her ashore. The woman returned alone and found the old woman, who was just questioning her. The woman told her everything and offered the dates to the old woman. Then, the beautiful woman disappeared without a trace. The woman could not find her. She asked many people, and they passed the message around, but there was no good news. At the time, it was widely rumored that she was a water deity, perhaps a dragon maiden or a fairy. After several months, the woman passed by the old woman again, but the old woman was not there. The woman tried to ask for details, but no one answered. She flew away gracefully. Whether she was a fairy, ghost, or demon, it was impossible to know.

### Translation **Fan Zhongyan as the King of Hell** During the Xinchou year of the Chenghua era, a minor official named Wang Jing from Changzhou died from illness but revived after one day. When asked about his experience, he recounted: "At first, I was gravely ill, and a spirit official pursued me. I saw a king sitting on a throne in a grand hall, with officials and clerks calculating something. I secretly asked others what they were calculating, but they did not answer. I then eavesdropped on what the king was saying. It turned out they were calculating the salaries of scholars. The officials finished their calculations and reported that the senior scholar had only a few months left, while the junior scholar had only a few more days. They then called me forward, and the king was surprised, saying, 'Mistaken! This is not the Wang Jing we are looking for.' He immediately sent me back. I then secretly asked others who the king on the throne was. Someone told me it was the King of Hell, Yan Luo. I asked, 'What is his title and surname?' They replied, 'He is Fan Zhongyan, the Censorate Minister of Suzhou.' I then woke up. At that time, both the scholar and his father were still alive. Later, the scholar, Liang Chen, fell ill and died. After a long time, the high-ranking official passed away. Upon examining the timing, it was found to be without a moment's error." --- **Wang Shu** In my village of Di Bian, there was a man named Wang Shu who once lay alone in his study. As the night approached its midpoint, a ghost howled in front of him. The sound was like that of a duck. Wang Shu, upon hearing it, was not afraid and said, "You howl if you want to, but I won't listen to you. Just don't come near my bed and disturb my ears." The ghost then made a sound like a goose. Wang Shu laughed and said, "It's just this kind of sound." The ghost did not leave and then made a sound like a chicken flapping its wings, hoping to scare him. Wang Shu said, "I will sleep deeply and not listen to you." The ghost insisted on disturbing him and covered Wang Shu with his bed curtains. Wang Shu said, "I just felt a bit cold, and covering me is very appropriate." The ghost could do nothing about it and stopped making any more noise.

### 乡人妾 A villager bought a concubine because his wife was fierce and placed her in a separate house. He set a date with her, saying he would come on a certain night. When the time came, he forgot about her. The concubine waited for a long time without him coming, and as night was dividing into two halves, she heard the sound of knocking on the door. She quickly got up, but the lamp had already gone out. She opened the door and asked her husband, but he did not speak. They both went into the room and slept together, but they said nothing at all. The concubine also began to suspect, and after the matter was over, she got up and left. The next day, just before dusk, the husband came, and the concubine greeted him and said, "Why did you leave so suddenly last night?" The husband was surprised and said, "Last night, I was stopped by some matter and forgot our previous agreement. How can you say such a foolish thing?" The concubine's face changed color and said, "I had indeed suspected it." So she told him the reason. The husband said, "Tonight, we will not sleep and sit together to wait for it." By around the second watch of the night, from afar, he saw a white dog from his home standing on its hind legs with its head held up. He then immediately killed it. The concubine also had no other explanation. ### 蒋令作土地 The magistrate of Leping County, Jiang, was previously noted for being a harbinger of success in the imperial examinations. After taking office, he did not survive even one year of examination. On the evening when his successor arrived at the office, he saw a ghostly figure of Jiang in official robes sitting upright in the main hall. He was startled and asked, and the figure replied, "My name is Tinggui, and I am the former magistrate. I died without committing any wrongdoing and should be worshiped in this land. I hope you will consider this." The new magistrate agreed and built a temple on the outskirts of the county. The local people began to worship the county's land god with great reverence. Jiang was known for his intelligence, kindness, and integrity, and he had a sincere faith in the gods, so perhaps this is why it happened. ### 乙未地震白毛生 In the year of Yinwei, during the month of April in the year 1485, the city of Wu experienced a major earthquake. In the morning, they found that white hair had grown all over the ground. The white hair was like the whiskers of a cat, several inches long, and the wind blew, making the white hair move gently. After two days, the white hair suddenly disappeared. My family managed to collect a few strands that remained unchanged for a long time, and they are still preserved today.

The Old Man of Hua In the Hua clan of Xishan, there was an old man who was quite well, having lived past the age of fifty. One morning, as he leaned against the door, gazing idly, he suddenly saw a tenant arrive at his home, carrying a cloth bag. The tenant placed the bag on the ground and greeted him with a respectful "yes." The old man just replied when he suddenly remembered that the tenant was already dead. He asked, "You are already dead. How can you be here?" The tenant replied, "I am indeed dead, but now I am here as a public person." The old man was even more astonished and asked, "Why?" The tenant explained, "After death, one becomes a public person, and I have been summoned to capture your soul." The old man said, "Then are you here to capture me?" The tenant replied, "Not yet. I am part of a batch of over fifty people. I fear you might not know, so I came first to inform you before going to the others. After the task is complete, I will also have a month to settle your affairs and leave calmly." The old man asked, "What is in your bag?" The tenant said, "I was afraid to scare you, so I did not take it out." The old man insisted, and the tenant poured the contents out on the ground. They were all bull-headed, horse-faced, fanged, and fiery-eyed creatures, made of ritual paper. The old man asked, "What are these for?" The tenant said, "In the underworld, all ghost officials or those summoned for tasks use these paper figures to disguise themselves. They are like the statues and paintings in the human world, but even non-ghosts can use them, merely dressing them up with this paper." The tenant added, "There is also someone in the east of your house who is in the same batch. I am going to summon them now. Would you like to go with me to see it?" The old man agreed. They went to the tenant's home, where the person was ill. When the family saw the old man, they all rushed to greet him respectfully, inviting him to sit and offering tea. The old man tried to stop them, but they did not understand his intentions and also did not see any ghost. When the ghost arrived at the door, he immediately took the ritual paper, put it on his head, and entered the room. Suddenly, there was a loud noise like firecrackers in the air. The family was startled to find that the sick person had already died. The old man then returned home, settled the affairs, and informed everyone in detail, bidding them farewell. Everyone was relieved. Soon after, the old man gradually fell ill for over a month and then died.

Zhou Qifeng During the Tianshun era, there was a man named Zhou Qifeng from Jiangyin. He was ingeniously cunning, unparalleled in his skills, and possessed a myriad of talents. He was deeply interested in esoteric techniques, performing supernatural transformations that dazzled and terrified the senses, yet he indulged in unscrupulous deeds. Consequently, people all feared and avoided him. After his death, a friend of his once summoned a divination tray, and Zhou Qifeng suddenly appeared. He moved the tray as swiftly as a flying bird, speaking hundreds of words in an instant. His poem went as follows: The Milky Way slants, stars scattered across the sky. The water clock drips, nearing the third watch of the night. A half-moon hangs small in the heavens. The path through the Three Mountains is veiled in mist and darkness. I am enjoying wine in the Three Mountains. I entered to inquire about my old friend who has been ill for a long time. In a moment, an imperial decree arrived. The Three Mountains labor to send me swiftly like the wind. Last night, I prayed to the gods, but they did not respond. My old friend is still on the move. The divine power is still within me. Where is my old friend, and why am I troubled? Recalling when we met in the past, we often met in the wind and moon of Anping. My heart is not happy, and no one knows. I wish to go to the Golden Platform and present myself at the Golden Hall. Who knew that fate would lead to my downfall. Alas, I cannot return to my hometown. Tonight, under the moonlight, holding a bright candle, I talk about my past life, which is full of sorrow. Remembering the time I spent in the study, the scholars were close and accompanied me in solitude. We spent twenty years together in a relaxed manner. Alas, our playful times turned into tears. The Autumn Official and I will maintain a casual acquaintance.

A Lament for the Fallen Heroes and Reflections on Life Who should provide coffins for the fallen heroes whose bold spirits linger even in death? Their sorrowful souls wander aimlessly, unattached to any place, as they traverse the vast mountains and rivers. The setting sun and the fragrant grass of late spring evoke a sense of melancholy, and one cannot help but listen to the cuckoo's cry, which seems to mourn the fallen. Reflecting on the days spent in humble dwellings, there were moments of regret where words were difficult to express. At that time, one's own actions seemed unkind, and words that went against reason also led to unreasonable consequences. Now, let us not speak of past events, for time flows like water, and flowers fall, just as the moon waxes and wanes. The two distinguished gentlemen, Mr. Yin and Mr. Xi, are exceptional guests, elegant and refined in their demeanor. Even Wang Xizhi, the master of calligraphy, is not enough to compare to them. The rare and precious things, like jade from Kunlun, are worthy of mention. A smile at the altar reveals one's true character, but this is not the time to discuss the past and present. What should one do on such a beautiful night? Let us compose poetry to exchange, and you may sing while I listen to your song. I cannot bear to hear it, for tears flow freely. In the capital, the days have passed, and I am pleased to share news with my relatives. We recite poetry late at night, and I wish for safety and well-being. The elderly man, who suffers from a condition caused by gastric cold, should take a medicinal remedy. The prescription includes loquat leaves, white cardamom, perilla seeds, and other herbs. With sincerity and faith, one can achieve their goals, even if the outcome seems uncertain. I desire to return to a spiritual realm, playing the flute three times. The gods grant me the opportunity to cultivate and compose poetry. Who is the poet this night? It is the wandering scholar Zhou Qifeng from the rivers and lakes.

Se Junnan's Dog Se Junnan, whose full name was Shen Jinaan, was the younger brother of Mr. Shen Weiming. He was exceptionally talented but died young. His family had a dog that was quite unusual. I once wrote an article titled "The Strange Dog" about this dog, which said: The Strange Dog was a dog belonging to Mr. Shen Weiming. Initially, when Mr. Weiming's father, Mr. Jinaan, was buried at home, several hundred steps away, they later moved east to a new place, four li away from the old grave. When the new grave was being built, the dog happened to follow them once. After the grave was completed, the dog disappeared for several days and nights, causing the family to be puzzled. In the evening, they observed the dog, and it was seen floating in the water outside the gate, heading east. They followed it, and the dog sometimes ran and sometimes swam, crossing three rivers and four streams before finally reaching the new grave. It crouched and lay down quietly and peacefully. This continued for many years, and the dog remained unchanged despite the weather. When I passed by Mr. Weiming's path, he told me about the dog. He called the dog to come and showed it to me. The dog was black with white fur between its neck and feet, and it was only four feet long. Mr. Weiming also said that this dog often accompanied Mr. Jinaan to Taicang. Once, when the boat was docked, the dog accidentally got off the boat. When they set sail again, all the servants forgot to look for the dog. They only realized it when they returned home and found the dog there. The distance from Xiangcheng to Taicang is about eighty li, and the dog's nature was indeed spiritual.

The Concubine of Officer Qian Officer Qian of the Shan family, residing in the capital, sought a concubine who served him with utmost care and respect. Qian greatly favored her. Later, he sent someone to bring his wife to the residence. Upon her arrival, Qian did not even look at her, nor did he acknowledge her presence, even when she sat beside him or passed by his side. It was as if she were invisible to him. The wife believed that Qian had been bewitched by the new concubine and had abandoned her, and she did not question it. The concubine, when leaving the room, always locked the door carefully without fail. After more than a month, one day, Qian entered the concubine's room during the daytime. The concubine had accidentally forgotten to lock the door. Qian saw a faint light from a small lamp stand on the floor, about several inches tall, with a lamp burning dimly. Next to it was a bowl of water, and a mouse was tied to the foot of the bed. The mouse was fed delicious food in a container, making it very fat. Its eyes were covered with a cloth and nailed shut. Qian was greatly alarmed, quickly extinguished the fire, poured water over the mouse, killed it, and threw it into the latrine. Shortly after, he encountered his wife, who exclaimed in surprise, "How did you come here? Where were you during this time?" The wife replied, "As if you didn't know?" For more than a month, you didn't pay any attention to me. Qian said, "I didn't even see you." The wife then explained in detail, and Qian was even more shocked, saying, "I didn't even see you. And I have already seen my mother, yet I didn't even think of you. Why didn't you come with me? Now I understand. The concubine has bewitched me. I am in danger!" He immediately called the concubine forward, dismissed her, and resumed his happiness with his wife as before.

**Final Translation:** The Art of the Courtesan The practice of *yase* (厌胜) is widespread, especially among women, maids, and courtesans in the north. There was a young man who became intimate with a courtesan because of her beauty and wealth, and he benefited from her in many ways, serving her with great attention and fearing to lose her favor. The young man was deeply entranced and stayed at her home for a year. Even though other courtesans, who were more beautiful and talented, could not sway him. One day, while lying in the daytime in a room with a window, he ordered her to go to the market to buy fish for lunch. Soon after, he saw the courtesan carrying the fish herself into the room. He privately wondered why she didn't send a maid and instead carried it herself. He became suspicious and began to observe her closely. The courtesan was unaware that he was watching her. She carried the fish directly into the toilet. The young man grew even more suspicious and curious, leaning against the window to watch carefully. He saw the courtesan place the fish in an empty chamber pot and leave. Shortly after, she brought another vessel and poured something into the chamber pot, which looked like water but had a different color. He quickly looked down and saw that it was menstrual blood. He felt intense hatred and disgust, called her to say goodbye, and left without eating. According to *Bo Wu Zhi* (慱物志), it is said that if menstrual blood is placed at the threshold of a door, women will linger. It is said that by burying menstrual blood at the threshold, women entering the house will stay and not leave. This shows that the saying is indeed true. Not only can women use this to keep men, but men can also use it to tie women. This practice has been around for a long time. It is also said that if a courtesan does not want to receive someone, she will sprinkle salt into the water and throw it into the fire, and the person will feel anxious and leave.

Illusionary Arts The late official served as a governor in the Jin state and often witnessed illusionary arts. Sometimes the performer would drink several cups of vinegar, or lie naked on his back with a large stone pressed against his chest and abdomen, or heat stones and scales until they were red-hot and place a pot in his mouth. Even women and children could perform such tricks. When guests arrived, a woman put wheat in the mill and cut out a paper donkey to turn the mill and grind the wheat, then quickly put the donkey back into her sleeve. When the woman was drawing water, the guest became thirsty and asked for water. She then gave him a spoonful of water. The guest looked around and found no one to lift the woman, not knowing that she had a trick. The woman smiled and invited the guest to stay overnight. The guest happily agreed, but the woman did not come. In the morning, the guest saw that his donkey was in a stone enclosure, with its neck too large to fit through the small opening; it is unclear what trick made it enter. The guest was very embarrassed, and the woman laughed happily beside him. She taught the guest to apologize and beg, and the donkey came out on its own. Village children herded cattle, sheep, donkeys, and horses without any weapons to protect themselves. They scattered the animals across the fields and let them roam freely. They gathered sand and drew a large circle with their fingers, and the animals stayed inside the circle. The children slept soundly, even if tigers or wolves came this way; they would only crouch around the outside, drooling but unable to enter. At dusk, they opened the circle, and the animals returned safely. Such incidents were common and too numerous to count.

Wang Chen In the year Renyin of the Chenghua era, there was a sorcerer named Wang Chen, originally from Xiangyang. As a youth, he was wayward and as he grew older, he wandered aimlessly across the land. It is unknown how he came to possess knowledge of heretical practices and sexual techniques. He then entered Nanjing via Huai and made connections with various high-ranking eunuchs, who brought him to the imperial court. He was granted the rank of Thousand Households. Wang Chen boldly spoke of alchemical matters, claiming that gold could be created instantly, and that the necessary ingredients were mostly found in Jiangnan. He wished to go there to collect them, and thus the emperor ordered the powerful eunuch Wang Jing to accompany him. Wang Chen was sent to the provinces directly under the emperor's jurisdiction, as well as Jiangxi province, where he requisitioned treasures from the treasuries and seized vast amounts of wealth from wealthy households. He traveled through Suzhou and Hangzhou, where his demands were especially severe. He issued a document listing the names of people in the marketplace, seizing their gold and jade. No one was spared, and some even fled with their families, closing their doors during the day. Passersby on the roads falsely spread rumors that his disciples were coming, causing the market to empty as people hid. The southeastern region was in turmoil, facing a great disaster, and the local officials were powerless to stop it. Some even closed their doors and dared not handle official business. He also ordered his disciples to copy imperial edicts, and when they slightly followed his instructions, he became even more demanding, leading the students to fiercely criticize him, nearly beating him. He then hid in an inn and reported the students for disobeying imperial decrees and assaulting the imperial envoys. Relying on the repeated reports of the governor, Minister Wang Shu, who exposed his treacherous actions, Wang Chen was pursued and brought back to the capital, where he was executed. His head was sent to Jiangnan, and the various regions that suffered from his actions were left devastated.

### Final Translation #### Yuan Shangbao's Physiognomy During the Ming Dynasty, Yuan Tingyu was unparalleled in the world in the art of physiognomy, almost like the heavenly net. His son, Yuan Shangbao, was also exceptionally skilled. My maternal grandmother, Lady Qian, and my uncle, Mr. Mengshu, were both virtuous and wise. Uncle Mengshu taught in Ningbo at the beginning of the Zhengtong era. When my father had already passed the local examination and went to pay respects to Yuan Tingyu, Mr. Mengshu ordered him to visit all the students in the schools and schools as well as my father. Yuan Tingyu examined each one individually but said nothing about my father, even when asked. At the time, we did not understand his intention. After several days, Yuan Tingyu came alone to visit my father and gave him two personal seals. One was inscribed with the words "Censor's Seal," and the other with "Governor's Seal." Later, my father indeed held both positions without fail. My father also told me that when Yuan Tingyu was in the capital, he gathered with officials at the home of a scholar who was about to host a banquet. The officials asked Yuan Tingyu about their prospects, and Yu Shi Kou, the Minister of Justice, asked particularly. Yuan Tingyu specifically told Yu Shi Kou, "Please go home and deal with urgent matters, then come back to ask me." Yu Shi Kou hurried home, but his house had already been burned to the ground. #### Zhou Qilang During the Yongle period, my family in Suzhou's Loumen hosted a wedding feast and invited friends, including Zhou Qilang. Zhou was registered in the textile dyeing bureau. At that time, a high-ranking eunuch came to oversee the bureau and suddenly arrived in the county, gathering all the workers. Zhou was the only one who did not come. The eunuch became very angry and sent dozens of people to bring him. My family immediately captured Zhou and recorded the names of all the guests present, reporting it to the emperor. The emperor was furious and sent a military officer to summon the host and guests to the capital, without asking for any explanation. They were all handed over to the eunuch supervisor and imprisoned in a single room, starved for ten days. The military officer said they would be released the next day, but by the time the deadline passed, eight or nine of them had died, leaving only Zhou alive. On the ninth day, Zhou was extremely weak. At night, the guards carefully checked and locked the door before leaving, and Zhou survived. ---

卧于床将三皷倐见一人自床边出近周前，周强开目视之，乃其故父也。昏昧之极亦不省其鬼父，捧饭一大碗兼有羔火肉之娄页，谓周曰：“吾儿可快餐之。”周恍惚间接而食尽，父乃不见。洎天明，周觉体渐强，又告监者欲起登厕。监者惊曰：“汝何得今日反胜于昨乎？必有弊。”周曰：“公视检门窻，纵有人何处入邪？”监者亦无可诘，而期巳满，乃即引奏。周竟得生还，调息数日始复云。 孔镛星 吾乡孔公名镛，字韶父，宣圣五十八代孙也。人忠孝弘毅，行巳唯以一诚可以质诸神明，不忝圣冑，乡邦增重久矣。平生政绩不可胜书。今岁己酉之秋，以都御史自贵州巡抚召工部侍郎，行至杭之富阳驿，少驻命驿吏视风色，忽觉瞑眩，从者急扶入舟，遂奄然而薨焉。将薨而有火起于舟上，甚大而烈，暨公之气渐微则火光渐高而渐小，迨于气绝则光巳在天际凝定烨煜，宛然一星矣。余谓自传相之后，载籍中贤臣多矣，而未闻有踵骑箕之列者，今而得公，殊足钦仰。穪之孔镛星可矣，余纂怪牒濒竟得此，遂谨以尾吾书，以览者假重于公焉。
志怪录 (明) 祝允明 志怪虽不若志常之益然幽诡之事固宇宙之不能无而变异之来非人寻常念虑所及今苟得其实而纪之则卒然之顷而值之者固知所以趋避所以劝惩是亦不无益矣况恍语惚说夺目警耳又吾侪之所喜谈而乐闻之者也昔洪野处夷坚志至于四百二十卷之富彼其非有乐者在则胡不中辍而能勉强于许久哉吾以是知吾书虽芜鄙不敢班洪亦姑从吾所好耳若有高论者罪其缪悠而一委之以不语常之失则洪书当先吾而废吾何忧哉

陈僖敏俞宫保 陈僖敏公镒与太子太保俞公士悦少时相得出入恒俱一日黎明同入郡学傍一居人寝门内闻街上枷锁之声窥之见狱卒自变量囚南行俄而卒及囚皆仓皇曰丞相来宜急避之遂皆入古庙而没居人念囚不应行此路且讶丞相之语因坐以伺之则二公随至矣 还娘 泰和萧都宪子妇刘氏年十七归萧字还娘忽得疾死萧氏从地里家说未及旬即葬之郭外还娘初入冥府见王者三人坐顾还娘曰误矣柰何吏白王欲姑留之王执不可遂放还途间见邻妇亦追来还娘问之妇曰以女擅杀一鹅棰之至死其诉冤追至耳还娘既生在棺中无计以出忽隐隐闻冢上羣鸟鸣噪有人见而怪之至墓旁闻墓中号呼之声告萧氏家乃急发冢出之瘁疲巳甚便加救治渐以强爽得安计死十七日矣方邻妇棰婢时还娘巳死数日以是知其不妄
鬼买棺 成化壬寅春吴中疫疠盛行田野尤甚五澴泾有一家七人同死无孑遗无人敛市有函人遇一老诣门买棺七具而赤手不持纤价函人索之其人曰汝但载我并棺到家当还汝也函人遂载棺与俱去将至门其人曰我先归开门待汝家无一钱惟有麦二十斛可以偿汝屋后西北某家予亲也可我召来言毕遂先登岸入门函人乃与舟子至其舍则寂无人焉视室内有尸七而买棺者在其中大骇出门觅其邻果有西北居某人其亲也语其故亲亦惊哀来酬价果有麦二十斛乃俱与之

天堕草船 松江城西有董仲俯素以敦厚穪成化丙午八月十一日天宇澄霁皎无纤云众见空中有小船从东而西又转而东堕仲俯楼上观者塞道细视之乃茭草所缚者时仲俯方患耳瞶亦不大惊但曰此船来载我耳不久果卒 白犬怪 昆山一民家子方少年未娶夜有一羙妇来与之合由是得瘵疾医疗莫愈子犹秘其事一日坐肆中有道士过之谓人曰此子妖气甚浓言毕遂去其父闻之乃谓子曰吾儿不言即当死矣言之吾不责汝也子乃以实告父曰吾以剪刀与汝伺其来但剪其衣便可验治子受命惟谨其夜妇至子密剪其衣亟厉声斥之妇遂去旦视之皆白毛也父见僯家一牝白犬毛有缺处因共杖杀之子疾随差

冯副使 吾乡冯副使定成化中赴京师听选朝罢趋归径至宗人府府中有古井甚大冯忽下马趋井旁顾其中拱揖数四从人及见者方惊视冯倐然觧带置地上随涌身而下急救之巳死矣冯在途间唤一吹手卒在舟喜其善觱栗携之至京卒恳求去冯怒棰之致死至是人以其报云 贡尚书 成化十六年罗洗马璟李侍讲东阳同典南畿秋试李在院梦有神谒云贡尚书来见李延之乃一贵官貌甚伟袍笏肃然谓李曰仆有小孙颇读书今来应试愿录之李逊谢既觉以告罗且素念不闻有此人漫不意巳而取舍初定李又梦前贵人来谢曰小孙巳荷收拔特此伸谢遂寤犹不能觧比拆卷首选乃宁国贡钦宴间李问之寔前元尚书师泰之孙也 王捉鬼 相城村人王郎素有胆气尝夜启门暗中见一物疑其鬼即前擒之大呼曰鬼在此家执火视之乃一朽棺板也王立烧之声如爆竹良久乃乡人号其人曰王捉鬼

海神请读书人 嘉定东门外有朱外郎子年十三岁一日挟书囊将就外傅倚门少立忽向东疾奔其去如风瞬息不见父母急集族里追之无及也路旁人云适奔过抱树求止不能得树折径去矣又至前人告如初俄而人报云直望海而趋矣继报云巳入海矣父母无所施计伏水滨恸哭三日将还忽水中涌出一人视之其子也方将抱持子向西仍去如风父母急回至家无有也又皆痛骇明日有丐儿来报曰昨夜 卧前村土地堂见一童子伏地而睡视之实尔家郎君也可往取之父母惊喜急与丐儿同往则果在地扶掖以归犹昏昏然不省娄页中恶者幸而眼开能运动以汤液灌之一日始复众问其详曰儿方倚门忽见一少年从东来貌甚娟秀戴软趐唐帽衣绿袍东黄金带骑白马亦莹皎如雪从后者可三十人皆人身而首则或虾或螺或鳖或鱼不一娄页少年见我即命从者羣挟之东行虽大呼不可得止也

至岸视海中一道水开遂成路接沙众皆拥上路四望弥漫俄顷至一城郭入至大宫阙朱门华屋弘敞焕烂少年止于门外只令阍者通谒曰奉命请读书人至其阍者亦鱼鳖之属受语趋入少顷复出命他吏引入至一殿下其殿极高广都作白色似蠵蛎之壳所光采照耀恍惚夺目不能正视吏命入殿王者坐殿上其貌巳老须鬓雪白其冠亦娄页唐帽身披白袍通剌金纹腰横白玉带问我曰汝觧 作文章乎对曰不能曰然则何能曰只会作对王曰我只要作文章者今汝不能无用汝也因命左右引入学馆一观即放回左右遂引入东偏一室见一童子可数岁韶秀特异旁吏云王欲请读书人教此子耳少顷复引见王王命仍遣人送去众复引出前少年尚待于门得传命因复乘马命众扶之归至土地庙前庙神出迎甚恭少年即以儿付之土地即收儿宿儿后亦无他今尚在也事在成化十九年

雷书 丁未五六月吴中大旱河底生茂草赤地千里至七月二十五日忽大雷雨势烈甚其日申时崇义庵东邻张氏有梓树合抱雷击一大枝既而视树下其地上有字横径五六寸长贰尺余画纹如指阔深入土几寸其文曰子乃言非篆非草虽经雨水冲灌而不湮没天神书也张氏子予友次日予始知之以纸摹得其文 妓乘鱼 南京教坊一妓与一盐商情好甚密商行货广陵语妓曰我不久即归汝能待我乎妓诺之商一去不来妓门户寥落犹无改念但多方访商巳而得其所在遂驰往寻之果遇焉商感其意因赠之金帛甚富曰我事未毕尚得几日始行汝姑回此物亦不足尽吾意俟归与汝续前好也妓买舟独归数取金令舟人碎之买物舟人窃念可图至江中夜静即

投妓水中尽有其货妓至水即有一物乘之安稳如坐径行去日将出至江阴一村大呼救我村中集人视之顷刻抵岸下众扶掖而起视所乘乃大鱼妓告其事众共送至县治官之出捕问其验妓言船之状且云有红罗鞋一只上绣石楼花并绣主腰一件同置舟中席下枕旁计此时贼尚未见也官如其言急往捡诸船果获盗寘之于法而觧妓还院云云 保保 常熟之福山有农家生一子名保保肢体柔弱四五岁犹不能行终日坐木榻上善言人祸福叩之皆应其家固怪之然亦因以致饶裕一日有龙虎山道士过其门语人曰此家必有妖物富人刘以则闻之召道士问之曰若能除之乎道士曰此不难也刘遂具舟使往道士方行保保忽告其母曰有一道士来儿当死矣可与钱十千儿乞命母方怪之道士巳在途潜书朱砂符焚于道旁柳树根下保保巳在家呕血又行不久再焚一符则有一鳣死于水面长壮可畏其首大如拳问其家子亦死矣

腹裂生子 凤阳府宿州民张妻王氏有孕当产脐下之右痛不可忍凡历三月惫甚成化十年三月初一日亥时腹之右边忽开裂一处产得一男其鼻凖中有一黑痣官给养廵按御史周蕃闻之于 朝予亲见周云云 莲花和尚 蜀中有一大桥侧有僧居焉僧素谨愿善无怠暇则织草履挂桥上过者欲取则取之推此他可知也一日雨下霹雳大震视之则此僧脑裂而毙矣咸以误或疑其有隐慝但心腹不冷其徒未殓五日则复苏曰始被击时既死雷神惊曰误矣亟令取药治之又令众鬼揉合破处久之复完好乃还生不知死巳五日矣视其头皮虽连而骨巳碎作八楞状人因呼之莲花和尚吴叟在蜀目击其事 天裂 先公说宣德中在学舍一日以事赴郡中当未申时忽见天裂于西南凡十余丈时晴碧无翳见其中苍茫深昧不可穷极良久乃合时同辈及衙前后数百人咸见之

谢老牛 苏州阊门外上塘杨家以鬻油业畜一牛行磨凡十八年矣以其老而衰议卖之召一人与论价将成交夜托梦于其人曰我施巷谢挑盘也因负杨家债作牛补还之今既老主人将卖我与君切勿买也其人梦觉不经意而杨氏亦不来召既数日又梦前牛曰主人明日召君矣必以后日杀我我与君素无雠万勿买也又曰我在杨家第四栏中身有白毛颈皮以行磨久巳皱破君当认之言讫而泣其人乃许之天明方共叹咤杨氏果来召去纵观其牛至第四栏宛然梦中所见者见其人来即屈双足于地泪下被面杨大怪之其人因语之故更劝杨勿杀杨亦大惊感誓不敢卖也而谢氏子知之遂来赎去养之终牛之身时成化十八年春也 何老人卖鬼羊 长洲有何老人者尝入城赴役天尚未明途中见一羊何疑之曰深夜安得有羊此必鬼也负之以行不少置羊哀鸣求释何不顾天明视之羊也遂诣肆卖之得银伍钱晚归复寻买羊人问之其人云旋失之矣何以实告而返其银

叶宗可 叶宗可元末避地淮阳时寇至积尸满野叶宗可昼伏夜行至前途度不能行则卧地上杂众尸中至夜分明月下遥见一人来烛笼前导近而视之一道士也一童子执烛偕至尸旁道士以烛烛群尸凡妇人老翁幼稚羸尫残癈者俱不用以手提而掷之轻如一叶俄得一壮男子骸体魁硕道士细视之有喜色乃即觧衣与之合体相抱持对其口呵气入其中良久道士气渐微尸冉冉动俄而欠伸又开眼遂推道士于地蹶然而起立仍令童子执烛前导飘然而去不知所之 张道士 苏城玄妙观雷殿道士张宗茂平生谦谨和易习行五雷法甚灵每遇水旱郡县必召之祈祷立应成化甲辰授道纪司都纪予尝有文赠之丁未正月即移病闲居然固亡恙也至十六日归俗家徧访诸族属曰宗茂老矣此行乃作别耳既乃入城出访交游或以别言人不喻也又语其徒曰老子欲去矣至五月一日晨起盥濯极絜持香诣殿上拜礼毕曰我今日辞雷公去矣俄入舍捡衣裳

杂物分遗诸徒迨夜乃默然坐小室榻上至明日封香帕诣各房请见众道士曰宗茂今日拜辞诸君矣回舍即危坐不语诸徒侍立不去师令出既而视之师瞑目矣亟大呼救师开目曰今日甚好汝辈勿留我也可将姜汤来我欲饮众兢去觅汤师亟起闭户乃上床坐而化去良久弟子来呼之巳不醒矣时十六日巳时也留一偈曰六十四年明月其中有圆有缺今日飞上太空三界十方透彻 刘西江 永乐间有刘西江先生好游一山寺僧颇厌之寺有空室二间人宿者多死一日西江至寺僧乃盛馔享之此室劝之抵醉就宿焉二皷酒醒闻诵观音经其声渐近西江以僧徒耳揽衣起坐时牎外月色如昼西江自牎隙窥之乃一羙妇人也诵毕即向牎合掌作礼牎忽自开妇人从牎入便据上坐俄而吐舌长丈余将逼西江西江仓皇以被扑之妇人若呕哕状遂去不见蚤起谢诸僧去行过山侧见一巨蛇吐舌而死西江曰此蛇精也即夜来妇人耳非我必彼一饱还以告僧僧愧谢之

杨礼部父梦 杨礼部循吉未第时其父梦人告郎君当中五十四名既觉不以意巳而中乡试会试廷试皆得一十八名谓前梦不足凭矣后追思之以三举之数合之即五十四也 尊经阁 苏郡庠之尊经阁建自宋代甚弘固相传阁上有祟人罕得登宣德中有无赖子与人誓约夜独寝其上及明无事则众当出金畀我众从之其夕无赖独处于阁夜半闻阁下有呵导声窥之则五丈夫冠裳楚楚从者亦都二笼烛前引登阁无赖急伏梁上视其所五人者危坐正面从者即奉酒馔铺列案上肴醑果核丰腆精絜饮器皆黄白错落满案鸡鸣将散无赖因呼噪以惊之诸祟一时奔逸都无所见器物狼籍案上不暇收拾无赖大喜过望尽怀其器以下众方来踪迹之无赖以实告众方骇叹俄传乐桥线铺钱氏宵间失去金银酒器若干无赖谓诸人曰此岂钱氏物乎持之诣钱钱视之果其家物也钱富而喜悉举以归之

文秀才 吾苏文秀才宗严名森尝夜读书于楼上忽闻人登楼声须臾一美妇立几前宗严兀坐不动遽取砚击之应手而逃无所见后文官至都御史 测字 往年有叩试事者书串字术者曰不特乡闱得儁南宫亦应高捷葢以寓二中字故也一生在旁乃亦书串字令观术者曰君不特不与宾兴当更得疾询其所以曰彼以无心书故当如字汝以有心书串下加心乃患字耳巳而皆然 鬼告状 外大父徐武功公治水张秋时有一铁匠役罢而归过岳王庙侧逢一故识匠悟其死唾骂之鬼大怒与之相扑久不觧忽公前驱至匠欲告鬼鬼挽之同匿河滨匠强拽出鬼力持牢不可脱曰我何敢见徐相公我且回避但可见三司官耳巳而三司官随至匠乃大呼鬼随与俱出三司驻节视之匠以两手作势虚抱曰鬼在此鬼作声云此人负我钱六百三十文今必还我有刘佥事者问匠曰有诸乎匠曰有之刘乃谓鬼曰铁匠既负汝钱吾汝追还汝可急放之鬼即听命而刘复谕匠匠回多焚镪以酬鬼巳而平安

蒋君科第前兆 余母姨夫蒋君廷贵应试时求江东签得词云前三三后三三初不晓所谓巳而三举不利成化辛卯宿旅邸中梦本学教谕陈裕率之观华屋甚且指谓之曰此即今年试题也蒋徐思之葢富润屋之谓也遂加研究既而果以诚意全章命题遂中第三名刊此篇义文云初蒋君梦后染疟甚笃时时惊呼同邸生问之则曰吾见三鬼来扰吾耳一日又骇跃大噪问之则曰适又见一羙姝谓我曰我乃桂花仙耳汝勿怖也人曰此吉征矣洎临场强扶舁以入既中思之无不应验三鬼者人以第三名经魁之应也及上南宫又三举始中进士第三甲益见签语之异两三三字上皆指科数下则指名甲殆无一字虚设云

朝天宫道士 弘治元年春南京朝天宫一道士年甫弱冠忽患膝痛视之皮肉皱缩然痛不甚极至夜则痛加倍视之乃人面疮也耳目口鼻靡不具足愁骇之极遇人辄与观之冀得所治然每有人观必痛入骨髓人不观则复不痛其昼间披衣行动饮食畧无苦至夜便剧虽无观者亦痛忽见二三尺许长一人立其前与之言曰汝识我乎道士曰不识也曰汝岂不记在宋都统制乎我则汝所部也我无罪汝以私憾杀我且并我一家十七口皆毙此何等冤也今觅汝凡三百年矣幸而遇汝我岂能舍汝哉道士遂卒

吉昌 成化末嘉兴民家生男背有红纹如血缕隐隐是吉昌二字其家因以儿乳名后数月村民发地得青石板上有字曰吉昌版下窖有银数百锭每锭亦各凿吉昌字乡民数十争取之儿父亦在曰吾儿身有吉昌字且其名也欲尽得之众不从特让其多取殆十巳五六家遂大富余人得者疾病官讼缠绵不巳或以贸迁亦自消耗一人颇有识曰此诚天付彼儿非吾物也举而归之其它亦从之罄所有畀儿父由是数家得无恙儿今犹在襁褓中未卜其后如何也 南京匠人 成化末南京一匠人赴内府工罢晚出三山门唯有一弊裘兼粗衣一二事钱三十文作一幞负而行至地僻处遇一人曰兄何往匠曰将取某路还家其人曰我亦欲由此路归昏暮可偕行匠甚幸与同入饮肆又买酒同饮匠唯感慰既而共宿翌日一同至极寂处忽推匠仆地拳之蹴之匠出不意弗能起御所痛殴遂陨绝推堕大隍中奔而去至前

途开幞视之唯弊衣少钱耳意甚悔到家复开幞掷之床上弊裘悬梁上钱亦姑置几下俄而风雨大作匠在隍中气未绝闻风声稍醒爽见一神人告以贼姓名居处及钱幞所在又语以贼意且悔今在家不出某官即将过此汝当呌号速往捕之必得矣语既匠醒果有廵逻官卒至匠大呼官出而问之得其说径往扑贼与衣钱等所在无少差遂正其法 长桥羙人 吴江垂虹桥旁有妪素业柯斧兼粥裙钗弘治初年秋日妪偶出市一小女留守室中时斜阳映门女倚门少玩湖光瞥见一羙人从小青衣迤里徐步女方属目羙人径入其家女叙拜延坐谛视之犹未笄而姿色艳冶妆饰瑰丽风态飘逸恍惚倾人颇若贵家处子女不敢详叩羙人便问婆婆安在对少出羙人曰我不能待当重来相访也出门掩舟而去妪归女告之妪莫测亦不意明日妪出羙人复来女惊接谢之曰娘奉候久不见临顾有事又出矣羙人曰唯女乃询其姓氏不荅又问小娘子有何言幸遂言之羙人低回久之掩袂羞涩作语语

女曰我要相烦求一配耳女曰然则候娘回当告之羙人曰幸存心勿忘也因拉女曰我居处非遥幸送我去女便随之同行稍久见画舫舟羲堤旁羙人谓曰此吾舟也率女偕登舟中张设珍丽莫伦异香清辉顿殊人世中有一小几几上有枣一盂羙人取数枚奉女女啖一二留二于怀羙人又送登岸女乃独归见妪妪方诘责女具陈其事出枣奉妪既而杳不复知稍物色之不得也以诘诸人好事者转相诹访绝无好音时哄传以水仙殆蛟娥龙女之辈矣既数月忽又过妪又不在女力叩其详终不见答翩然而逝仙邪鬼邪妖邪不可得而知也

范文正公阎罗 成化辛丑长洲泾小民王敬病死一日复生问之云初病笃有冥吏追之见王者坐殿上判官方与吏胥运筭敬窃问旁人筭何事皆不答敬因潜听王者所言乃筭商学士俸禄吏筭讫覆云大学士尚有数月小学士只有月余既而引敬问之王者惊曰误矣非此王敬也急放回敬又窃问旁人殿上王者谁或告曰阎罗王也曰何官何姓也曰即苏州范参政仲淹也遂寤时商公父子俱亡恙既而学士良臣病死久之阁老乃卒审其时无少爽焉 王鏄 吾邑荻扁里王君鏄尝独卧斋中夜将半有鬼啸于前其声娄页鸭鏄闻之无所惧但云汝呌自呌吾不管汝但勿近吾床聒吾耳也鬼乃作鹅声鏄笑曰不过是此等声耳鬼终不去复作鸡鼓翼声庶几其一惧鏄曰吾且熟睡不听汝矣鬼必欲动之遂以其床帷覆鏄身上鏄曰吾适寒覆之甚宜鬼无如之何不复作声

乡人妾 乡人买一妾因妻悍置之别馆与期曰某夜当来及期忘之妾久伺不至就寝夜将分闻叩户声亟起而灯巳暗中开门问其夫夫不言苐共入室遂同寝合焉终无一言妾亦稍疑之事竟即起去明日薄暮夫来妾迎谓曰昨宵何去之遽也夫惊曰昨夕以事阻遂忘前约汝何言之谬欤妾色变曰吾固疑之矣因道其故夫曰今夜当勿寝同坐以伺之至二更许遥见家中白犬以前两足捧其头作人立而来乃即格杀之妾亦无他 蒋令作土地 乐亭县令蒋君乃前所书科第先兆者既作邑然未及一考而卒后代者来入廨之夕恍惚见蒋君公服危坐正寝惊问之荅曰廷贵姓蒋即旧尹也死幸无过当血食兹土冀明公图之新尹遂诺立庙于县治隙地县民穪县土地颇崇香火蒋平生聪明长厚行履忠信有不欺神明之志或应有此理也 乙未地震白毛生 成化乙未春四月吴城地大震旦视之遍地皆生白毛正娄页猫须长数寸风过冉冉而动两日忽无有余家收得几茎历久不变今尚存焉

华老 锡山之华富族也有一老颇平善年逾五十一旦方倚门闲眺忽见一租户至其家手携一布囊及门置于地声喏曰小人在此华甫应之忽忆其巳亡问曰汝巳死矣何得至此对曰固死矣今正公来耳华益惊曰何曰身死得公人今奉命勾摄华曰然则摄我乎曰未也某一批凡五十余人某恐公不知首至此奉报然后他之计勾完亦得一月公可处置家事从容而去华曰汝囊中何物曰恐相惊不敢出也华强之因倾出于地则皆牛头马面獠牙火 眼之属悉是色纸所华问安用此曰冥间凡鬼公人或摄事则假此物笼戴诚如阳世所塑画者然非鬼身亦有此特以此纸具装之耳又曰宅东某亦同批今往勾之公欲同往一视否华从之行至其家某者方病家人见华来咸奔走致敬延坐烧茶华固止之其家不测来意而亦不见鬼使也鬼至其门外即取色具笼戴之入其室便闻空中如爆竹一声其家方惊则病者巳死矣华即回步至家处分后事且详告人致永诀人皆宽释之既而渐病月余而死

周岐凤 天顺间有周岐凤者江阴人巧黠无与比身兼百技溺意方术神怪变幻眩骇耳目而恣不肖之务故人皆畏而远之既死友人偶召箕周忽至运箕如飞顷刻数百言其诗曰 银河斜斜星斗横铜壶漏滴将三更一天明月半轮小三山路窅烟冥冥我在三山乐樽酒入问故人淹病久须臾符命至三山劳我凌风速于走昨夜告神神不灵我故人行不停灵方肘后我还有故人何处劳心情追忆当年曾会面安亭风月常相见我心不乐人不知欲赴金台奏金殿谁知天数竟沦亡吁嗟不得归故乡今宵月夜秉明烛又说前生可伤长记留连在芸屋诸公相亲伴幽独从容交处二十年可怜嬉戏成悲哭秋官与我且淡交出

帑何当赠棺木英豪壮气死还存何事凄凄在鬼录孤魂飘飘无所依万里山川人不归夕阳芳草暮春候忍听杜宇花稍啼番思昔寓涂菘日一时念错言难出此时侬家太不仁言悖出兮亦悖入如今往事且莫谈水流花落月当三殷奚二公非常客风流儒雅皆青衫云间士龙何足数昆冈片玉可伍一笑坛前识表仪未是相从论今古月明如此良夜何作诗相赠君且歌君歌我诗不忍听两行清泪空滂沱汉明芸屋陶良玉京都及今几回禄宗范举 人喜无事我传言与亲属朗吟坛前夜欲阑丹青写与期平安菊壮老人此老病翻胃病实由胃寒枇杷叶兮白荳蔻紫苏子兮莫用谬良姜官桂用些须厚朴陈皮功奏半夏槟榔白茯苓沉香丁皮皆用轻白芥藿香吐圣药杵头糠兮寻至城三片生姜两枚枣切切分明向君道人参乃是佐使药食前一服沉痾好土地顾公也是神君有诚心莫负盟君有诚心祈必应纵然虚诞何须评我欲归兮笛三弄神仙假我修洞题诗今夜是何人江湖散人周岐凤

沈继南家犬 相城沈继南名召启南先生弟也秀粹而蚤夭其家一犬颇异余尝作异犬传曰异犬者沈惟明家犬也始惟明先人继南甫之葬在家而数百步既乃东迁亟宇圩去故垄四里甫治冢时犬偶随一往及冢成犬数宵失旦复家人怪之昏时相伺察犬见犬浮门外水而东即尾视之犬或走或游踰三四川径奔冢前跧伏帖帖而卧如是累年霜雨无变焉比过惟明道犬事呼犬至示余犬正黑色颈足间白而长仅四尺先生又说此犬常随继南往太仓舟停时犬偶登岸比觧维诸仆都忘觅犬至家方怒惜之少顷犬亦来家乃陆行而归也相城至太仓且八十里犬性亦灵矣哉

钱御史妾 钱山御史者在京师觅一妾侍奉惟谨钱甚嬖之既而令人往家迎其妻来比至钱见妻全不留目虽妻逼坐其侧或从妻身畔行过亦不少顾若无人焉者妻直以惑溺新宠故弃巳耳亦不问之其妾出室必谨锁室门畧不懈弛居月余钱白昼间入妾房妾偶忘扃锁钱见妾床下有火光惊视之则一小灯檠高数寸上燃灯荧然其旁有水一盂缚 一鼠于床足置羙食于器中饲鼠甚肥以布幕其两目仍加铁钉焉钱大骇亟火覆水杀鼠掷之厕俄而逢其妻惊曰娘子何自来许时在何处妻曰若佯不知邪月余弗吾顾也钱曰我何曾见汝来妻乃告其详钱益惊曰吾目中并不见汝且巳见吾母亦竟不思有汝在何不同来今始悟耳乃知妾厌胜吾吾其危哉立唤妾前谴而黜之与妻欢乐如初

娼家厌术 厌胜之术在在有之其在北方妇人群婢娄页多能此而娼家甚焉有少年郎狎一娼妇以其羙且富也利之百端趋奉惟恐失意郎惑甚留其家巳经岁虽他娼才貌兼胜者弗能移也一日昼卧楼窻下命往市鱼午餐俄而见娼自携鱼以入私念彼胡不使婢辈而必自持邪注意察之娼不知郎之察巳也提鱼径入厕中郎益疑怪附窻谛窥之 见娼置鱼于空溺器中而去顷之又将一器物注溺器中若水而色异亟下视之乃月水也便大恶恨召与言别竟不餐而行焉余按慱物志有云月布在户妇人留连注谓以月布埋户限下妇人入户则自淹留不肯去则知斯言果可信且不特女可以此留男男亦可以此系女也其来久矣又闻娼不欲接其人则撮盐入水投火中其人便焦急而去

幻术 先公仕晋藩每见幻术或饮醋数升或祼体仰卧以巨石压胸腹或煅石秤捶通红而口行缶之至如妇女小儿亦有能者客至妇以麦置磨剪纸驴运磨得面旋复收驴入袖一妇方汲水过客渴索浆既勺与之客顾无人挑妇不知其有术也妇笑留客宿客喜从之妇则不至而旦视其所骑骡以首枷入石栏中矣项大栏小不知何术使入也客窘甚妇喜笑于旁或教客谢过恳祈妇始复施术骡自出矣村儿放牧牛羊驴马之娄页畧无兵仗以卫但散置郊原而纵之惟聚沙土以指周施画一大圈畜处其中童亦酣睡任有虎狼至此惟蹲踞环绕于外垂涎而巳不能入圈也日夕开画安然而归若是之娄页皆以常不可胜纪

王臣 成化壬寅有妖人王臣者本襄阳人少亡赖长而流荡四方不知何自得左道兼晓房中术遂由淮入南京结纳诸中贵人因以上闻召入京师得千户臣肆言炉火事黄金可以立成诸药多出江南愿往采取遂命大珰王敬偕行臣直隶诸郡及江西省地征发府库箕敛富室金帛累巨万展转至苏杭科需尤甚信意出一纸录市人姓 名括取金玉人无得免或挈室而窜白日闭户途路行人妄传其徒将来则市人空肆而匿东南骚然有娄页大变郡县无如之何亦或闭门不敢治事又命吾学徒抄录命书既稍从之复发无巳遂诸生大诟詈几欲棰之乃匿于驿亭上奏诸生违敕殴使臣等事赖廵抚尚书王公恕累疏陈其奸状追逮回京伏诛仍传首江南被害诸郡

袁尚宝相术 国朝袁廷玉相术独步天下几如天纲而其子尚宝忠彻亦精绝无伦先祖妣淑人钱氏伯父孟书先生绅正统初掌教于鄞先公巳领乡荐往省之时袁家居先生令遍视庠序诸生以及先公袁一一决之而于先公独无一语叩之亦不答于时未测其意越数日袁来独访先公手赠私印二枚其文一曰给事中章一曰参政之章后先公竟拜二官不爽先公又说袁在京时与朝士同集一士人家将合燕诸公就袁叩升沉俞司寇亦在问之尤切袁特告俞曰公且请归家治忙事郄复来问俞即驰回其庐舍巳被火几烬矣 周七郎 永乐间吾苏娄门陈氏以婚礼燕客友人周七郎与焉周隶籍织染局时巨珰口行缶命来督局事猝至郡便拘集诸役人周独不至珰大怒遣数十人就往陈氏即席擒周兼录座客姓名以闻上怒复遣校尉特来通提席间宾主赴京不问其故都付宦者令囚于一室饿十日来说既而不及期先后死以八九独周一人乃不死至第九日惫甚及夜监者谨点捡扃锁而去周

卧于床将三皷倐见一人自床边出近周前周强开目视之乃其故父也昏昧之极亦不省其鬼父捧饭一大碗兼有羔火肉之娄页谓周曰吾儿可快餐之周恍惚间接而食尽父乃不见洎天明周觉体渐强又告监者欲起登厕监者惊曰汝何得今日反胜于昨乎必有弊周曰公视检门窻纵有人何处入邪监者亦无可诘而期巳满乃即引奏周竟得生还调息数日始复云 孔镛星 吾乡孔公名镛字韶父宣圣五十八代孙也人忠孝弘毅行巳唯以一诚可以质诸神明不忝圣冑乡邦增重久矣平生政绩不可胜书今岁己酉之秋以都御史自贵州廵抚召工部侍郎行至杭之富阳驿少驻命驿吏视风色忽觉瞑眩从者急扶入舟遂奄然而薨焉将薨而有火起于舟上甚大而烈暨公之气渐微则火光渐高而渐小迨于气绝则光巳在天际凝定烨煜宛然一星矣余谓自传相之后载籍中贤臣多矣而未闻有踵骑箕之列者今而得公殊足钦仰穪之孔镛星可矣余纂怪牒濒竟得此遂谨以尾吾书以览者假重于公焉
